evidence_quotes.quote: Dark patterns in online interfaces that can unfairly influence their decisions, for example, by putting unnecessary pressure on consumers through false urgency claims.
Addictive design of digital services that pushes consumers to keep using the service or spending more money, such as, gambling-like features in video games.
Personalised targeting that takes advantage of consumers' vulnerabilities, such as showing targeted advertising that exploits personal problems, financial challenges or negative mental states.
Difficulties with managing digital subscriptions, for example, when companies make it excessively hard to unsubscribe.
Problematic commercial practices of social media influencers.

paragraphs.text: Hello! First of all thanks for letting me be able to send in my feedback to you, and thanks a lot for you work! My name is Anders Andersson and I live in Sweden. I follow the Youtube channel AccursedFarms on the Internet who is trying to raise awareness for the "Stop Killing Games" movement, and he informed me and his viewers about the "Digital Fairness Act". Personally I have a massive Steam library which could theoretically be removed any day, games I bought to be able to play. There is probably online requirements for even single player games that may or may not work depending on servers somewhere being online and responsive. From what I understand the movement is that it tries to change legislation such that once a company want to stop supporting a game there should be an exit strategy for the community so they can continue play the game they have bought. For instance, sending a patch that removes the online requirement so when the company who created the game can shut down their servers and the game will still function to the best it can considering the circumstances. I think digital preservation is very important. It is our culture, it will inspire future generation to create new games. Just as we can listen to music, look at art and read books from creators generations ago, so I want digital creations be accessible to future generation. There is countless of examples on Internet of users using Internet Archive to view previous works on Internet. Why shouldn't we be able to also start old games and be able to play and experience it again. But the trajectory is going towards streamed, rented and online only games regardless if the Internet requirement makes sense or not (e.g. why would a single player game have to be connected to Internet what so ever, Sim City 5 had this requirement and almost all modern big studio games have that requirement). According to my source (https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=E6vO4RIcBtE) you are working with similar issues and it would be great if "Stop Killing games" could be added to your cause as well as it is very similar to your existing great list of suggestions for change. Thanks for all your hard work, it is really appreciated! The topics you already cover is just as important and I really would like to say that I stand behind you on all these points: 1. Dark patterns in online interfaces that can unfairly influence their decisions, for example, by putting unnecessary pressure on consumers through false urgency claims. 2. Addictive design of digital services that pushes consumers to keep using the service or spending more money, such as, gambling-like features in video games. 3. Personalised targeting that takes advantage of consumers' vulnerabilities, such as showing targeted advertising that exploits personal problems, financial challenges or negative mental states. 4. Difficulties with managing digital subscriptions, for example, when companies make it excessively hard to unsubscribe. 5. Problematic commercial practices of social media influencers. Some of these practices may already go against existing EU consumer law and other EU law, for example, the Digital Services Act and the Audiovisual Media Services Directive.
